fuzzy_prd:
hey team, so we need to build out this terminal table rendering thing for the ride comparison CLI. basically travelers are complaining that the current output is really hard to read — prices from different providers are showing up in inconsistent formats, and when multiple cars have the same pickup time it just lists them separately which wastes screen space and looks cluttered. we want something clean and box-drawn like those fancy terminal UIs.

the renderer needs to handle two views: one for pricing comparisons and one for wait times. for pricing, it should show each option with its fare, how far the trip is, how long it takes, and whether there's any price surge happening. for wait times, it should group options that arrive at the same time together instead of repeating rows.

we talked about this in the last sprint — remember how we handled the multi-currency stuff in that billing display module? same idea here, different currencies in the same list should each show their own symbol. also the time formatting should work the same way across both views, consistent with how we did the duration display in that earlier feature.

borders should use that gray styling we agreed on. the whole thing should be a library with clean separation — not just one big file dumping everything to stdout. input comes in as a structured object and you get back a finished string. invalid inputs should fail gracefully.

trigger_keywords: what does surge column look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When surgeMultiplier is exactly 1, display the 🚫 symbol (no-surge state). When surgeMultiplier is greater than 1, display the multiplier value followed by 'x' and then the 😬 emoji — for example '2.5x 😬' or '1.1x 😬'. The surge column header is ' 💥 Surge💥 ' and always occupies 10 characters of inner width.

The fare range is formatted as '<symbol><low>-<symbol><high>' with no spaces around the dash. USD maps to '$', GBP maps to '£', EUR maps to '€'. Each option resolves its own symbol independently from its currencyCode field. For example USD with low=10, high=14 renders as '$10-$14'.

Duration is humanized as follows: less than 60 seconds renders as '<n> sec.' (e.g. '4 sec.', '30 sec.'). Exactly divisible by 60 with no remainder renders as '<n> min.' (e.g. '5 min.', '10 min.'). Both minutes and remaining seconds present renders as '<m> min. <s> sec.' (e.g. '1 min. 30 sec.'). This same humanization rule applies to both the price table duration column and the wait-time table time column.

trigger_keywords: how to show miles or kilometers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Distance is rendered as '<value> mi.' for MILE units and '<value> km.' for KILOMETER units. The numeric value is rounded to at most two decimal places. For example value=1, unit=MILE renders as '1 mi.' and value=9, unit=MILE renders as '9 mi.'.

trigger_keywords: how products are grouped in time table
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the wait-time table, products sharing the same estimateSeconds value are collapsed into a single body row. Their names are joined with commas (no spaces around commas) in the order they appear in the input array. Distinct wait times produce separate rows, ordered by first-encounter position in the input. For example if 'jae' and 'bae' both have estimateSeconds=1, the products cell shows 'jae,bae'.

trigger_keywords: what color are the table borders
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All box-drawing border characters (┌, ─, ┬, ┐, │, ├, ┼, ┤, └, ┴, ┘) are individually wrapped with ANSI gray escape codes: \u001b[90m before each border segment and \u001b[39m after it. Cell content between borders is NOT colored. This applies to both the price table and the wait-time table.

trigger_keywords: start end location in price table
location rows at bottom of price table
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The price table ends with two footer rows below the last option row. The first footer row shows a 📍 pin pictograph in the left cell and the start location name in a merged right cell spanning the remaining four data columns. The second footer row shows a 🔚 pictograph in the left cell and the end location name in the same merged right cell. The separator above the first footer row uses ┼ and ┴ characters to close the upper portion of the data columns while keeping the left column open.

trigger_keywords: how the location name is displayed at top
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The wait-time table has a full-width banner as its very first row (above the column headers). This banner spans both columns and centers '📍 <locationName>' within the full inner width of the table. The two-column pictograph header row (⏳ on the left, 🚘 on the right) appears below this banner, separated by a ├┬┤ junction row.

Column widths are determined by the widest cell content in each column across all rows (including the header pictograph row). Each cell is padded with one space on the left and enough trailing spaces on the right to fill to the column width, then one more space. The surge column in the price table always has a minimum inner width of 10 to accommodate ' 💥 Surge💥 '. Pictograph characters count as 2 display-width characters each.

trigger_keywords: what icons appear in price table header
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The price table has five columns with these header pictographs (centered with padding): 🚘 for product name, 💸 for fare range, 🔃 for distance, ⏳ for duration, and '💥 Surge💥' for the surge indicator. The wait-time table two-column header uses ⏳ for the time column and 🚘 for the products column.

Invalid inputs — including unknown mode selectors, unknown distance units, or malformed payloads — must be surfaced as a normalized, language-neutral error category rather than leaking host runtime fault details such as stack traces or exception class names. The execution adapter is responsible for catching these and returning a clean error string.

The codebase must be organized into a src/ tree with separate units for: value objects (ride option data models), formatters (currency localization, distance formatting, duration humanization, row grouping), and table builders (price table renderer, wait-time table renderer). The execution adapter (stdin/stdout + JSON parsing) must be kept completely separate from core rendering logic. Do not collapse all logic into a single file.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the multi-currency stuff in that billing display module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The currency symbol resolution logic in the fare-range formatter: USD→'$', GBP→'£', EUR→'€', applied independently to both the low and high bound of each option's range, as specified in feature1_3_currency_range.json and the CurrencyFormatter/similar module under src/formatters/

context_pointers.vague_reference: the time formatting should work the same way across both views, consistent with how we did the duration display in that earlier feature
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The duration humanization function is shared between the price table (duration column in seconds) and the wait-time table (estimateSeconds column). The rules are: <60s → '<n> sec.', exact minutes → '<n> min.', mixed → '<m> min. <s> sec.'. This is visible in both feature1_2_surge_indicator.json (300s → '5 min.') and feature2_2_distinct_time_table.json (30s, 60s, 90s).

context_pointers.vague_reference: that gray styling we agreed on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ANSI escape code \u001b[90m (gray foreground) applied before each individual box-drawing border character or segment, closed with \u001b[39m (reset foreground) immediately after. Cell text content is never wrapped in color codes. Confirmed by examining the raw expected_output strings in any test case JSON file.

context_pointers.vague_reference: not just one big file dumping everything to stdout
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architectural NFR #1 and #2 from start.md: the execution adapter (JSON parsing + stdout writing) must be a separate layer from the core rendering library. The core API accepts a structured value object and returns a string; it has no direct dependency on process.stdin, process.stdout, or JSON.parse.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have documented on my end — you'll want to dig into the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at the expected_output strings directly to reverse-engineer the exact behavior. The test cases are the ground truth for any formatting question I can't answer offhand.

feature1_1_full_price_table.json.description: Render a full ride-price comparison table: a header row of pictographs, one row per ride product showing product name, localized fare range, trip distance, trip duration and a surge indicator, followed by two pinned footer rows for the trip start and end location names.

feature1_2_surge_indicator.json.description: Verify the surge indicator rule inside a price table: a product priced at the base fare (multiplier exactly one) shows a no-surge pictograph, while a product above the base fare shows its multiplier followed by a grimace pictograph.

feature1_3_currency_range.json.description: Verify the localized fare-range rule inside a price table: each product fare range is prefixed with the currency symbol that corresponds to its ISO currency code, applied independently to the low and high bound.

feature2_1_grouped_time_table.json.description: Render a wait-time comparison table with a centered location banner and a two-column body: products sharing the same estimated wait time are merged into a single row, their names joined by commas in ascending order of wait time.

feature2_2_distinct_time_table.json.description: Render a wait-time comparison table where every product has a distinct estimated wait time, producing one body row per product with no name merging, ordered by wait time.
